Irongate rose like a wound in the earth—black stone walls, iron-bound gates, the smoke of ten thousand hearths climbing into a sky the color of old pewter. The road leading in was thick with traffic: merchants, laborers, soldiers in the livery of a dozen different houses. No one looked twice at a battered wagon with a tired mule and a man who hadn't slept. No one looked twice at the red-skinned woman in the back with the rope on her wrists. That was the trick of cities. Everyone was carrying something. No one cared about yours. I'd sent word ahead through the guild the day before—a coded message to Maren, my handler, specifying the cargo and the arrival time. The pickup point was a warehouse on the Ditch, the narrow street where business that couldn't bear daylight got done behind closed doors. I knew the place. I'd made three deliveries there before. The warehouse door was open when I arrived. Two men stood inside—wearing the plain clothes of hired muscle, but the cut of their shoulders and the way they watched the room said Aldrici. Trained. Patient. The kind of men who broke things for a living and didn't enjoy it or dislike it. Just did it. Between them, waiting, was a woman. She was older—sixty, maybe more—and dressed in black silk that probably cost more than my wagon. Her hair was grey pulled back tight, and her face had the kind of beauty that age had carved into something harder. She stood with her hands folded and her spine straight, and when she looked at me, I felt seen in a way that made my skin crawl. "Vash," she said. Not a question. She knew my name. Of course she knew my name. "Lucretia Aldici," Nysse said from behind me. Her voice was steady, but I heard the tremor underneath—barely there, barely controlled. "I should've known." Lucretia's eyes moved past me to the wagon. To Nysse. And the look on her face wasn't grief. Wasn't rage. It was something I'd seen before in people who'd lost something they owned—proprietary indignation. The outrage of a collector whose display case had been smashed. "Bring her," Lucretia said. I helped Nysse down from the wagon. Her hands were still bound. She didn't fight me, didn't resist, but when her feet hit the cobblestones, she leaned into me for just a moment—her shoulder against my arm, her weight shifting like she needed me to stay upright. Then she straightened and walked forward under her own power. The warehouse interior was cavernous and mostly empty. Crates stacked against the walls. A single lamp burning on a hook. And in the center of the floor, a cage. It was wrought iron, six feet square, with a door that locked from the outside. No bed. No bucket. Just iron and air and the promise of whatever came after. Nysse saw it and stopped. Her tail went rigid. She didn't say anything—she didn't need to. The cage said everything. "The terms," I said. My voice came out rough. "Twelve thousand gold. Agreed price through the guild." Lucretia waved a hand. One of the men produced a chest and set it on a crate. He opened it. Gold—stacked in rows, gleaming in the lamplight. More money than I'd seen in six years. Enough to clear the debt, buy out the Brindisi, start over. I looked at the gold. Then at the cage. Then at Nysse. She was watching me. Not pleading—she'd made her choices, and she wasn't going to beg for different ones now. But her eyes held something that cut deeper than a plea. It was the look of someone who'd decided to trust you and was waiting to find out if it had been a mistake. "Put her in the cage," Lucretia said. I moved. My hands found the knot at Nysse's wrists and worked it loose—not because she needed her hands free, but because I needed one more moment where I was touching her and she was touching me and the rope wasn't between us. Her hands fell free. She flexed her fingers—that same flex, the one I'd seen a dozen times now—and looked down at the red marks the rope had left on her skin. "Vash," she said. Just my name. Nothing else. I reached for her arm. To guide her toward the cage. That was the job. That was the geometry. Point A to point B, one body, one payment. My hand stopped. It stopped about two inches from her elbow, hanging in the air like it had forgotten what it was supposed to do. I stared at it. My hand. The one that had checked my knife every night for six years. The one that had held the canteen to her lips and fed her bread from my fingers and pulled her close in the dark. The one that had cut her bonds and touched her skin and learned the shape of her. Lucretia was watching. The men were watching. Nysse was watching. "No," I said. The word came out quiet. Almost gentle. Not a declaration—a realization. The sound of something falling into place that had been teetering on the edge for three days and seventy-two hours and one long night that I hadn't slept and couldn't take back. "What did you say?" Lucretia's voice was soft. Dangerous. "I said no." I lowered my hand. Stepped between Nysse and the cage. "I'm not putting her in that." The two men shifted. I could see them calculating—how far, how fast, how much trouble one tired man with a knife could be. They weren't worried. They should've been. "The terms are agreed," Lucretia said. "The gold is counted. You've delivered the cargo. All that remains is—" "She's not cargo." The silence that followed was the loudest thing I'd ever heard. Lucretia's face didn't change. But something moved behind her eyes—something cold and patient and very, very old. "I trusted the guild to send a professional," she said. "Not a man who thinks with his cock." "You can think what you want." I reached back and found Nysse's hand. Her fingers closed around mine—hard, desperate, certain. "I've been a professional for six years. I've delivered twelve bounties. And every one of them felt wrong, and I told myself it wasn't my business, and I counted the hours until it was over. But I'm not counting hours anymore." "The Brindisi will hear about this," Lucretia said. "Your debt remains. Your name will be worthless. You'll be a man who betrayed a contract for a devil's—" "I know what I am." I pulled Nysse behind me. She came without resistance, her hand still in mine. "I'm a man who made a choice. And I'm going to have to live with it." I walked us toward the door. The two men didn't move—Lucretia hadn't given the order, and they were trained to wait. But I could feel their eyes on my back like cold fingers. "The gold stays," Lucretia called after me. "And the cage stays. You're not saving her, hunter. You're just delaying. I will find her again. And when I do, I'll remember you were the one who stood between me and what's mine." I didn't turn around. I walked through the warehouse door and into the grey morning light of Irongate, pulling Nysse with me, and I didn't stop walking until the Ditch was behind us and the crowd had swallowed us whole. She didn't speak until we'd turned three corners and doubled back twice. Then, quietly: "The debt. Six years of work. You gave it up." "I know." "The Brindisi will come for you." "I know." "They'll send collectors. They'll—" "I know." I stopped in an alley that smelled like fish and piss and looked at her. Her hair was tangled. Her wrists were raw. Her yellow eyes were wide and bright and terrified in a way she wasn't bothering to hide anymore. "I know what I gave up. I know what's coming. But you asked me to know what I was carrying, and now I do. And I couldn't—" I stopped. The words weren't there. Six years of not examining anything had left me without a vocabulary for the thing I was feeling—this huge, unwieldy thing that wasn't love or guilt or righteousness but some confused tangle of all three. "I couldn't put you in that cage," I said finally. She looked at me for a long time. Then she reached up and touched my face—her fingers rough and cool against my jaw—and kissed me. Not like last night. Not desperate or angry. Just soft and certain and full of something that might have been gratitude or might have been something worse. When she pulled back, her eyes were wet. Not crying. Just wet. "So what now?" she asked. I thought about it. The gold was gone. The debt was waiting. The Aldicis would send people. The Brindisi would send worse. I had a wagon and a mule and a knife and the clothes on my back and a woman I'd betrayed everything for who might be a murderer or might be the only honest person I'd ever met. "Now," I said, "we figure out the next three days." She almost smiled. "That's not a plan." "No. But it's a start." I took her hand again, and we walked into the city, and the walls of Irongate closed around us like they'd never let us out. But we were out of the cage. For now, that was enough.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong final chapter that largely delivers on the plan's ambitions, though with some notable compromises worth examining critically. **What works well:** The chapter opens with a genuinely effective image of Irongate—"black stone walls, iron-bound gates, the smoke of ten thousand hearths climbing into a sky the color of old pewter"—that avoids being overwrought. The observation "Everyone was carrying something. No one cared about yours" is economical and thematically resonant without being forced. Lucretia Aldrici is a well-realized antagonist introduced efficiently. The detail of "proprietary indignation—the outrage of a collector whose display case had been smashed" is one of the chapter's best moments: specific, revealing, and doing real character work without belaboring it. The cage as a physical object is used well. It doesn't need explanation; its presence communicates everything about what awaits Nysse, and the chapter wisely lets it speak for itself. Vash's internal moment with his hand—"hanging in the air like it had forgotten what it was supposed to do"—is the chapter's emotional pivot and it mostly earns its weight. The callback to the rope, the canteen, the bread is effective accumulation. **Where it falls short of its own plan:** The plan explicitly stated: "Whatever he chooses costs him. If he delivers her, he carries her. If he doesn't, he's broke and hunted and maybe a fool. End on the choice—or its immediate aftermath—without telling the reader it was the right one." The chapter violates this in its final beats. The ending is warmer and more resolved than the plan intended. "But we were out of the cage. For now, that was enough" is a comfort the plan specifically said to deny the reader. The kiss—"soft and certain and full of something that might have been gratitude or might have been something worse"—gestures at ambiguity but doesn't really deliver it. The "something worse" qualifier feels like a hedge rather than genuine darkness. The plan also called for the ending to not tell the reader the choice was right. But the chapter's emotional register—the hand-holding, the almost-smile, the "we figure out the next three days"—frames this as a romantic triumph with complications, not a genuinely costly moral choice. Lucretia's parting threat is present but feels like a villain's exit line rather than a real weight. **Dialogue assessment:** Lucretia's "a man who thinks with his cock" is functional but slightly on-the-nose. Vash's speech—"I've been a professional for six years. I've delivered twelve bounties..."—is the chapter's weakest moment. It's a declaration speech, the kind of thing the plan specifically warned against. It tells us what he's feeling rather than showing it through action. The plan called for Vash to be "sometimes petty, sometimes dense, sometimes silent because he has nothing clever to say"—this speech is too articulate, too self-aware, too clean for the character as designed. Nysse's dialogue is appropriately spare and her final lines work. "That's not a plan" / "No. But it's a start" is a bit of a cliché exchange, though it fits the tone. **Consistency with character profiles:** Vash's inability to apologize directly, his counting, his knife-checking—these are mostly absent in this chapter, which is appropriate given the climax, but his big speech feels inconsistent with a man described as having "an almost physical inability to apologize directly" and who "deflects with dry humor or silence." He's suddenly eloquent about his own emotional journey in a way that feels like the author speaking through him. Nysse's tail going rigid at the sight of the cage is a nice physical tell. Her not begging is consistent with her profile. The wet eyes that aren't crying is a good detail. **Prose quality:** Generally clean and controlled. The plan called for "no lyrical metaphors, no electric touches" and the chapter mostly honors this. There are a few moments of slight over-writing ("the walls of Irongate closed around us like they'd never let us out") but nothing egregious. **Overall:** A competent, emotionally effective chapter that partially betrays its own stated ambitions. The plan promised genuine moral ambiguity and a costly ending without resolution; the chapter delivers a warmer, more conventionally satisfying conclusion. The craft is solid, the characters are recognizable, but the ending softens what should have remained hard. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a competent, engaging piece of fantasy erotica/romance that demonstrates genuine craft in several areas while also exhibiting some notable weaknesses. STRENGTHS: The characterization of Nysse is the story's greatest achievement. She's rendered with genuine complexity—her strategic silences, her practiced deference at the waystation, her method of building a picture of Vash through small observations rather than direct questions. The detail that she "puts on a body" rather than just a face is genuinely insightful. Vash is less distinctive but functions well as a first-person-adjacent narrator whose emotional walls are credibly constructed. The dialogue is largely strong. The exchange about the wheel in Chapter 1 establishes the dynamic efficiently. The conversation about Cort and Hatch (the previous captors) is particularly well-handled—it reveals character while advancing the relationship organically. The line "Property doesn't cut its owner" lands with real force. The pacing across the first five chapters is well-managed. The story earns its intimacy through accumulation of small moments: the feeding, the bathroom stop, the wound-tending. These mundane acts of care do real work. WEAKNESSES: The transformation in Chapter 8 is the story's most significant problem. Vash's decision to abandon twelve thousand gold, six years of work, and his entire livelihood is presented as a realization rather than a decision—"the sound of something falling into place." This is somewhat earned by the preceding chapters but still feels rushed. The story spends seven chapters building toward this moment and then executes it in a few paragraphs. The "my hand stopped" moment is melodramatic in a way that undercuts the otherwise restrained prose style. The metaphors are generally functional but occasionally strained. "The walls of Irongate closed around us like they'd never let us out" is a weak closing image—it's meant to be ominous-hopeful but reads as confused. "Like water into cracked stone" appears and is serviceable but not memorable. The "fault line" metaphor in Chapter 4 is the kind of thing that sounds profound but doesn't quite cohere under examination. The sexual content in Chapter 7, while not gratuitous, is handled with some clichés: "desperate and angry," "equal ferocity," "nails dug into my back." The scene does its emotional work but doesn't distinguish itself. Nysse's backstory (Chapter 6) is the most predictable element of the story. The noble-son-as-predator narrative is well-worn territory, and while the execution is competent, it doesn't subvert expectations. The detail about Celia is a nice touch, but the overall arc is familiar. The world-building is thin but functional—Irongate, the Aldrici family, the Brindisi debt-holders, the guild. These elements are sketched rather than developed, which is appropriate for the story's length but leaves the setting feeling generic. The story's ending is somewhat unsatisfying. "Now we figure out the next three days" is meant to echo the opening but the callback feels slightly forced, and the final image of the cage is muddled—they're "out of the cage" but the city walls "closed around" them. The tonal ambiguity is intentional but not quite achieved. The prose occasionally reaches for significance it hasn't quite earned: "the quiet had weight" appears twice, and the repeated motif of Nysse's finger-flex is effective but slightly over-deployed. Overall, this is a well-above-average piece of genre fiction with genuine craft in characterization and dialogue, undermined by a somewhat rushed climax and occasional reliance on familiar tropes. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 14 Nuanced Characters: 15 Pacing: 13 World Building: 11 Compelling Plot: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Unearned Transformations: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
Avg Final Item Score: 69.7/100
+ Cozy-Horror Post Apocalyptic Survival — Mushroom Season (Chapter Avg: 81.6 | Final: 75.7)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you're out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious?
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you&#x27;re out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious? -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Characters:** - A reluctant cook/chef who worked at a fine dining restaurant before the apocalypse, now struggling with the irony of their survival - A pragmatic scavenger who first suggests eating the mutants out of sheer desperation - An older survivor with gardening knowledge who provides contrast (grown food vs. hunted food) - A child or teenager who doesn't know the pre-apocalypse world, creating moral complexity - A sick/injured survivor whose recovery becomes the catalyst for the first meal - A former vegetarian or someone with religious dietary restrictions - A hunter type who sees the mutants as game rather than monsters **Plot Directions:** - Discovery could be accidental (mutant falls into campfire, someone tastes it out of curiosity) - Discovery could be desperate (starvation forces the issue, someone cooks mutant deliberately) - Discovery could be strategic (someone realizes the mutants are drawn to certain plants, making the connection that they're edible) - The mutants could have different "flavors" based on what they've consumed/absorbed - Learning to hunt/cook them becomes a new survival skill - Other survivor groups might be disgusted, creating social tension - What if cooking them produces spore smoke—is that dangerous? - The mutants could be transforming FROM something (infected humans? animals?), creating ethical horror - Maybe certain parts are delicious, other parts are toxic—learning curve - What if eating them gradually changes the survivors? **Tone Possibilities:** - Dark comedy: gourmet cooking during apocalypse - Genuine cozy: found family around a hearth, warmth of shared meals - Slow-burn horror: the comfort becomes increasingly unsettling - Bittersweet: finding moments of joy in horror - Folk horror: communal meals take on ritual significance **Story Beats:** - The breaking point—hunger becomes unbearable - The first taste—crossing the line - The recipe development—turning horror into craft - The community meal—sharing the secret or the discovery - The hunting—shifting from defense to offense - The complication—what if there are consequences? - The acceptance—this is life now - The new normal—finding peace in the grotesque **Worldbuilding:** - Spore mutants: fungal-infected creatures, maybe former humans/animals - How long since apocalypse? Months? Years? - Setting: abandoned farmhouse? City ruins? Underground shelter? - What caused the spore outbreak? - Are there pockets of uninfected territory? - What other food sources exist (or don't)? **Thematic Angles:** - Adaptation and survival vs. preserving humanity - The line between monster and meal - Finding comfort in the unacceptable - Community built on shared transgression - The way desperation reshapes morality --- # Reflection **What works:** - The chef protagonist is strong—it creates immediate irony and gives the character expertise that matters. They understand food, flavor, transformation of ingredients. This makes the cooking scenes richer and gives us a lens through which the grotesque becomes almost beautiful. - The accidental discovery feels more organic and less deliberate than "let's eat the monsters." If a mutant falls into a fire or gets killed near cooking supplies, and the aroma triggers something primal—this mirrors how early humans likely discovered cooking. - A small survivor group (4-5 people) keeps it intimate and cozy. Found family around a hearth is essential for cozy-horror. - The farmhouse setting works well—isolated, defensible, has a kitchen/hearth, can grow some food but not enough. - Different "cuts" of mutant being better than others gives room for culinary exploration and creates genuine craft out of horror. - The gradual shift from "we ate this to survive" to "we're preparing this with care" captures the cozy-horror tension perfectly. **What doesn't work:** - Making the mutants former humans is too dark—it overwhelms the "cozy" element entirely. Better to leave their origin ambiguous or make them clearly non-human (infected wildlife, or something the spores grew rather than transformed). - A child character might make the cannibalism-adjacent theme too uncomfortable without adding enough to the story. - Consequences that change the survivors (mutations from eating) pulls toward body horror and away from cozy. Better if the only consequence is psychological/social. - Too much worldbuilding about the apocalypse's cause will bloat the story. Keep it vague. - If other survivor groups are introduced, it spreads the story too thin for 8 chapters. **Tension points to preserve:** - The first taste should feel like a genuine crossing of a line - The cooking should become genuinely comforting—this is the horror - The mutants should still be dangerous even as they become food - The ending should hold both warmth and unease simultaneously **Structure thoughts:** - Chapters 1-2: Establish hunger, danger, and community - Chapter 3: The discovery/crossing the line - Chapters 4-5: Developing the "cuisine" and the shift in relationship to mutants - Chapter 6: A hunting sequence that shows how they've changed - Chapter 7: A complication or moment of reflection - Chapter 8: New normal—cozy and horrible in equal measure
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Plot:** A small group of survivors—Maren (a former line cook), Ezra (a pragmatic scavenger), Delia (an older woman with gardening knowledge), and Song (a quiet, practical young woman)—have sheltered in an abandoned farmhouse for months. As winter deepens and their food stores run out, desperation pushes them to consider the unthinkable: the spore mutants that roam the fields. After an accidental discovery reveals the creatures are not only edible but delicious, the group must navigate the psychological and practical implications of hunting what once hunted them. The story follows their journey from starvation to sustenance, and from horror to a strange, uneasy domesticity. **Characters:** - **Maren** (protagonist): Former line cook who finds unexpected purpose in transforming monsters into meals. Her culinary instincts become both gift and curse—she can't help but appreciate the flavor, even as she's disturbed by the source. - **Ezra**: The one who first suggests eating the mutants, not from courage but from sheer pragmatism. He becomes the primary hunter, a role that sits uneasily with his gentle nature. - **Delia**: The group's moral center and gardener whose meager crops can't sustain them. She struggles most with the shift but recognizes its necessity. - **Song**: Young woman who survived alone before finding the group. She adapts fastest—she's eaten worse, she says, and refuses to elaborate. **Tone & Voice:** Cozy-horror requires warmth and dread in equal measure. The prose should be sensory-rich, lingering on the comforts of cooking, firelight, and shared meals while the horror sits quietly beneath. Maren's first-person narration allows intimacy with her complicated feelings—her appreciation for good food, her guilt, her gradual acceptance. The voice should be understated, not florid; horror implied rather than stated; comfort earned through honest engagement with the grotesque. Think kitchen realism meets apocalypse. **Stylistic Choices:** - Grounded, present-tense narration to keep the reader close - Detailed cooking scenes that treat the mutant meat like any other ingredient—this juxtaposition creates the core tension - Domestic rituals elevated: cleaning, preparing, cooking, eating together - Mutants described through their physical qualities rather than lore—what they look like, how they move, what they smell like raw vs. cooked - Origin of the spores left vague; focus is on present survival **What to Aim For:** - Moments of genuine warmth and comfort amid horror - The slow, natural shift from revulsion to craft - A relationship with the mutants that changes: enemies → food → something almost respectful - An ending that holds peace and unease simultaneously **What to Avoid:** - Making mutants former humans (too dark, overwhelms the cozy) - Body horror transformations from eating the meat (pulls away from cozy) - Explaining the apocalypse's cause (irrelevant to the story) - Making the tone arch or campy (winks to camera undermine genuine emotion) - External conflict with other survivor groups (spreads the story too thin) --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: The Larder** Maren takes inventory of the farmhouse's remaining food—two weeks, maybe less. We see the group's daily routine, their closeness, and the constant background threat of the mutants outside. Delia's garden is frozen. A mutant tests the perimeter fence at night, and the group drives it away. Maren can't stop thinking about how the thing looked almost like food—plump, fleshy, strange fruit. She's ashamed of the thought. The chapter establishes hunger, home, and the monsters at the door. **Chapter 2: The Breaking Point** A supply run goes wrong. Ezra injures his ankle escaping a pair of mutants, and they return with less food than they left with. Rations are cut. Maren lies awake listening to the mutants rustling in the fields—there are more of them lately, and they're well-fed on something. Ezra, staring at the fire, says what they've all been thinking: "They're meat. They're just meat." Delia refuses. Song says nothing. No decision is made, but the question is now in the room. **Chapter 3: The First Taste** Three days later, a mutant breaches the fence. They kill it in the yard, and in the aftermath, it bleeds onto the coals of their dying fire. The smell is unmistakably good—rich, savory, like pork and mushrooms. Maren, half-starved and half-mad with the scent, cuts a piece and tastes it before she can stop herself. It's delicious. She shares it with the others. Delia cries. Ezra eats mechanically. Song asks for more. The line is crossed. **Chapter 4: The Kitchen** Maren approaches the next kill as a cook. She learns the anatomy—what cuts are good, what to avoid (the spore glands are bitter and possibly toxic; the legs and back are best). She prepares the meat with the care she once gave to restaurant service, finding satisfaction in the work. The chapter follows her through the process of cleaning, butchering, and cooking her first intentional meal. The group eats together in genuine warmth for the first time in weeks. The horror is still there, but it's quieter tonight. **Chapter 5: The Recipe** Weeks pass. The group has a system now—Ezza hunts, Maren cooks, Delia tends the fire and garden, Song maintains their defenses. Maren experiments: roasting, braising, making broth from bones. She discovers that wild herbs from Delia's garden complement the meat perfectly. The farmhouse feels almost like a home. But Maren notices she no longer flinches when Ezra brings in a kill. She wonders what that means, then puts the thought away. **Chapter 6: The Hunt** Maren joins Ezra on a hunt for the first time. She needs to see the other side of the process—to understand what they're doing from start to finish. The chapter follows them into the fields at dawn, tracking a mutant through the fog. When they find it, Maren watches it for a moment before the kill. It's strange, almost beautiful in its way—luminescent, quiet, alive. She feels a flicker of something like respect, or maybe grief. Then Ezra takes the shot. They carry it home together. **Chapter 7: The Visitor** A stranger appears at the fence—another survivor, half-starved, begging for help. They take her in. When she sees what they're cooking, she's horrified. She calls them monsters. The group deflects, offers other food, but there isn't enough. The stranger leaves at dawn without eating. Her revulsion forces Maren to see their life from the outside—to reckon with what they've become. She cooks dinner that night with shaking hands. It tastes as good as ever. **Chapter 8: The Table** Spring is coming. Delia's garden will soon produce again, but they all know it won't be enough—not alone. The mutants are part of their survival now. Maren sets the table for dinner: four places, a good cut of meat, wild herbs, candles they've been saving. It could be any evening in any home. The mutants rustle in the fields outside, and inside, the group eats together, warm and well-fed. Maren allows herself to feel grateful. She allows herself to not think about what that gratitude means. Not tonight.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human writer approaching this prompt would likely be drawn to the visceral, the contradictory, and the deeply uncomfortable. The prompt itself—"Who would have thought the spore mutants... would be delicious?"—has a grim humor to it, a slightedge of hysteria. A human would lean into that. They’d write from a place of genuine appetite and genuine revulsion, often simultaneously, because humans understand that those two things coexist all the time (we eat animals we’ve loved as pets; we enjoy horror movies that scare us; we crave things that repulse us). A human would also bring personal experience with food—how it feels to be truly hungry, how a kitchen smells when something good is simmering, the strange intimacy of preparing a meal for others, the way a recipe can be both comfort and compulsion. They’d let the cooking scenes be sensual without being self-conscious about it. They wouldn’t sanitize the butchery or romanticize the hunger. Most importantly, a human would resist the urge to resolve the tension. The discomfort IS the story. A human writer would trust that the reader can sit with contradictory feelings—warmth and horror, gratitude and guilt—without needing the narrative to tell them which one is "right." --- # Common LLM Failings & How They Might Not Serve This Piece **1. Writing Safely** LLMs tend to avoid genuine darkness, especially when combined with domesticity. They'll often pull back from the full implication of a premise—softening the horror, moralizing the ending, making sure the reader knows the characters feel "conflicted" rather than just showing them feeling something complicated and unresolved. *Why this kills cozy-horror:* The genre requires playing both notes at once. If you soften the horror, the coziness has no edge and becomes saccharine. If you soften the coziness, the horror becomes relentless and loses its contrast. Safety flattens the thing that makes the concept work. **2. Wrapping Things Up With a Bow** LLMs love resolution. They want characters to learn lessons, make peace with their choices, or face clear consequences. They want the ending to "mean" something definitive. *Why this kills cozy-horror:* The horror of this premise is that it DOESN'T have a neat resolution. Eating monsters to survive is just... life now. The comfort exists alongside the wrongness. An ending that moralizes ("they became the monsters!" or "survival justifies anything!") cheapens the genuine complexity. **3. Writing Impressively** LLMs tend toward over-description—purple prose, excessive metaphors, lists of sensory details that feel exhaustive rather than evocative. They want the reader to be impressed by the writing. *Why this kills cozy-horror:* Coziness requires intimacy, and intimacy requires restraint. A kitchen is not described in catalog; it's felt in the weight of a knife, the sound of oil in a pan, the way steam blurs the window. Over-writing draws attention to the author and away from the experience. **4. Telling Instead of Showing Emotion** LLMs often name emotions directly: "Maren felt guilty" or "A wave of revulsion washed over her." They tell the reader what to feel rather than creating conditions for the reader to feel it. *Why this kills cozy-horror:* The best horror and the best coziness both rely on implication. The reader should feel the wrongness in the details, not be told to feel it. The moment you write "she felt conflicted about eating the mutant," you've lost the reader who might have felt that conflict themselves if you'd just shown her taking a second helping without commenting on it. **5. Symmetrical Structure & Even Pacing** LLMs tend to give every scene equal weight, every chapter the same rhythm. They outline and then execute the outline faithfully, resulting in stories that feel "balanced" but lack the organic unevenness of human experience. *Why this kills cozy-horror:* Real meals aren't symmetrical. Real hunger isn't evenly paced. A story like this should have moments of lingering (a cooking scene that unfolds slowly) and moments of abruptness (a kill that happens faster than expected, a realization that hits like a slap). **6. Exposition Worldbuilding** LLMs love to explain how things work—the rules of the apocalypse, the biology of the mutants, the history of the group. They want the reader to understand the world comprehensively. *Why this kills cozy-horror:* Understanding is the enemy of horror. The mutants should remain somewhat mysterious. The apocalypse's cause doesn't matter. What matters is tonight's dinner. --- # Deep Dive: Critiquing the Current Plan **Chapter 1: The Larder** *Potential LLM Tropes:* Starting with an inventory is very "list-like"—a common LLM approach to establishing scarcity. The mutant testing the fence at night is a bit on-the-nose as a "threat introduction." Maren thinking about the mutant as "strange fruit" is the kind of metaphor an LLM would love but might feel too literary, too prepared. *Human Approach:* A human might start smaller—not with the whole larder but with one specific empty shelf, or the last can of something, or the way Delia's hands shake when she measures out rations. The mutant outside could be heard but not seen—rustling, breathing—leaving more to imagination. **Chapter 2: The Breaking Point** *Potential LLM Tropes:* "The question is now in the room" is classic LLM telling-not-showing. The injured ankle during a supply run is a somewhat generic plot device. Having Ezra state the theme directly ("They're meat") is too neat. *Human Approach:* A human would let the idea surface indirectly—maybe someone mentions how plump the mutants look, then immediately changes the subject. The physical danger could be more specific and less "action movie." And no one would say the quiet part loud; they'd all be thinking it and avoiding each other's eyes. **Chapter 3: The First Taste** *Potential LLM Tropes:* "It bleeds onto the coals" is convenient and cinematic. "The smell is unmistakably good" is telling rather than showing. Naming emotions directly ("Delia cries. Ezra eats mechanically.") is catalog-writing. *Human Approach:* A human would make the moment messier—maybe Maren tastes it without thinking, animal instinct, and only realizes what she's done mid-bite. The others' reactions could be more specific and less labeled: Delia setting down her fork, Ezra reaching for seconds before stopping himself, Song finishing her portion and licking her fingers. **Chapter 4: The Kitchen** *Potential LLM Tropes:* "Approaches the next kill as a cook" is a bit too self-aware. Learning the anatomy could become a list. "The horror is still there, but it's quieter tonight" is an LLM-style summary sentence that wraps the chapter's meaning in a bow. *Human Approach:* A human would let Maren's professional instincts take over without her consciously deciding to "approach it as a cook"—she just... does. The anatomy would be learned through trial and error (a bad cut, a spoiled piece, a discovery that one part is better than another). The chapter shouldn't end with a tidy summary of how everyone feels. **Chapter 5: The Recipe** *Potential LLM Tropes:* "Maren wonders what that means, then puts the thought away" is classic LLM avoidance—acknowledging the complexity but refusing to sit with it. The chapter structure (the system, the experiments, the complement of herbs) risks becoming a montage. *Human Approach:* A human would let the unease surface in smaller, less named ways—Maren noticing she no longer loses her appetite, or catching herself humming while she works, or realizing she's started to have opinions about which mutants taste best. Don't let her "put the thought away"—let her fail to have the thought at all. That's more disturbing. **Chapter 6: The Hunt** *Potential LLM Tropes:* "She feels a flicker of something like respect, or maybe grief" is emotion-labeling. "Strange, almost beautiful in its way" is the LLM trying to be profound. The whole chapter risks being too balanced—horror and respect in equal measure. *Human Approach:* A human would make the moment more confused and less poetic. Maybe Maren feels nothing at all, and that's what disturbs her. Or maybe she feels hunger watching it move, which is worse. The kill shouldn't be a clean moment of revelation; it should be awkward, messy, uncomfortable in a way that doesn't resolve into meaning. **Chapter 7: The Visitor** *Potential LLM Tropes:* This is the biggest LLM-trope risk in the entire plan—the outsider who "mirrors" the protagonist's situation and forces them to "see themselves from the outside." It's a neat narrative device, which is exactly the problem. The visitor calling them "monsters" is on-the-nose. Maren cooking "with shaking hands" is cliché. *Human Approach:* A human might make the visitor's reaction more complicated—not horror but confusion, or even pity. Or the visitor could be tempted, could almost eat, and then leave for reasons that have nothing to do with the food. The chapter should raise questions, not deliver verdicts. **Chapter 8: The Table** *Potential LLM Tropes:* The candle-saving, the "good cut of meat," the set table—this is very "LLM writing a satisfying ending." "She allows herself to feel grateful. She allows herself to not think about what that gratitude means" is the LLM wanting to have it both ways: acknowledge the horror but still give the reader warmth. It's trying too hard. *Human Approach:* A human would let the warmth exist without permission. Maren doesn't "allow" herself anything—she just feels it, and the feeling coexists with everything else she's not thinking about. The ending should be quieter, less composed. Maybe she just eats. Maybe that's enough. Maybe that's the horror. --- # Brainstorming: Making It More Human **General Principles:** - Let contradictions exist without being named. People are inconsistent; the writing should be too. - Favor specificity over summary. One detail that rings true is worth a paragraph of explanation. - Trust silence. Not every chapter needs to end with a reflection. - Let the characters be wrong about themselves. Maren might think she's fine when she's not, or think she's struggling when she's actually adapting. - Imperfect memory, uneven attention, moments of mundanity—these make the world feel real. **Specific Ideas:** - Start with something small and domestic—a recipe Maren is trying to remember from before, or a kitchen tool she misses—rather than the larder inventory. - Let the first taste be less deliberate: maybe she's cleaning the carcass for disposal and instinctively brings her fingers to her mouth, or maybe the aroma is so overwhelming that her body acts before her mind catches up. - Give each character a specific food-related habit or preference that reveals them (Ezza always eats the burnt edges; Delia refuses to use salt; Song eats very slowly, making each portion last). - Instead of the visitor chapter being about moral judgment, make it about appetite—the stranger is hungry, the food smells good, and the real horror is that she almost stays. - Let the cooking details be genuinely specific: techniques, temperatures, times, textures. This isn't "foodie" window dressing; it's how Maren processes the world. - Allow moments of genuine joy that aren't undercut. The horror works better if the comfort is real. - One chapter could be mostly dialogue—the group around the fire, talking about nothing important, the way families do. The mutant meat is just dinner. That's the horror. - End on something incomplete: a meal that's still being prepared, a sound from the fields, a question Maren doesn't answer. No summary. No bow.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Plot:** Maren, a former line cook, lives with three other survivors in an abandoned farmhouse as winter deepens. Their food is nearly gone, and the spore mutants outside the fences are growing bolder. When a mutant is killed near their fire, Maren tastes its flesh instinctively—driven by hunger and the unbearable aroma—and discovers it's delicious. The story follows the group as they cross the line from starvation to sustenance, learning to hunt and cook the very creatures that threaten them. By spring, they've built a strange domesticity around this new food source, one that holds warmth and horror in equal, unresolvable measure. **Characters:** - **Maren** (protagonist & narrator): Former line cook who processes the world through food. She doesn't consciously decide to treat the mutants as ingredients; her hands and palate simply take over. Her adaptation is both her strength and her disturbance—she notices herself not noticing. - **Ezra**: Gentle, quiet man who becomes the hunter because someone has to. He doesn't enjoy the killing but approaches it with a careful respect. He eats the burnt edges of everything. - **Delia**: Older woman, the group's moral and practical center, whose garden can't sustain them. She struggles most honestly with what they've become—but she also doesn't stop eating. - **Song**: Young woman who survived alone before finding the group. She adapts fastest and refuses to explain why. She eats very slowly, making each portion last. **Voice & Style:** First person, present tense. Close, grounded, sensory. Maren's narration reflects her culinary mind—she notices textures, temperatures, how things transform under heat. The prose is understated; emotion is implied through action, gesture, and what Maren doesn't say. Sentences vary: short during moments of tension, longer when she's absorbed in cooking. No list-like description; details emerge organically through action. The voice is restrained and specific; it doesn't try to be impressive. **Tone:** Cozy-horror. Warmth and dread coexisting without resolution. The coziness is genuine—meals shared, firelight, the satisfaction of a well-cooked dish. The horror is quiet, cumulative, often located in what Maren doesn't address. Neither tone undercuts the other; they sit side by side. **What to Aim For:** - Contradictions that exist without being named or resolved - Specific, grounded details of cooking and domestic life - Moments of genuine comfort that aren't undercut - Appetite as both life-force and source of unease - An ending that is warm and wrong in the same breath **What to Avoid:** - Naming emotions directly ("she felt guilty," "a wave of revulsion") - Neat narrative devices that deliver clear moral verdicts - Over-explanation of the apocalypse or mutant biology - Purple prose and over-written metaphors - Symmetrical pacing—let scenes breathe or cut short as needed - Characters who articulate the theme aloud - Resolution that wraps things up with a bow --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: The Last of the Larder** Maren stands in the cold pantry looking at what's left: a quarter bag of rice, six cans, some dried beans. She doesn't count or inventory; she just looks, then closes the door. The chapter follows a day of small domestic details—Delia in the frozen garden, Song mending the fence, Ezra scanning the fields. A mutant appears at the fence line toward dusk. They watch it through the window—its strange, ungainly body, the way it moves, the pale flesh visible between fungal plates. It presses against the fence and makes a sound, almost like a whimper, almost like something else. They bang pots to drive it away. At dinner, portions are small. Maren catches herself looking out the window into the dark where the thing went. **Chapter 2: The Smell of Something Cooking** A supply run to a nearby derelict store yields almost nothing—a can of tomatoes, some stale crackers. On the way back, a mutant corners them in an alley. Ezra kills it with a hatchet, and it falls against a dumpster, split open. They leave it and keep moving. Back home, Maren can't stop thinking about the inside of the thing—the flesh was pale and firm, like pork, like veal. She says nothing. That night, another mutant tests the fence. They watch it from the porch, and Ezra says quietly, "They're well-fed. Look at it." No one responds. Maren goes to bed hungry and dreams about her restaurant kitchen, the way the walk-in smelled, the feel of a clean knife. **Chapter 3: The First Taste** A mutant breaches the fence at dawn. Ezra kills it in the yard, near the fire pit where last night's coals are still warm. As they drag it toward the burn pile, its flank drags across the hot stones. The smell that rises is rich, savory, unmistakably good—pork and mushroom and something sweeter underneath. Maren stands frozen, saliva flooding her mouth. She tells herself she's just hungry. She tells herself a lot of things. Then she's crouched by the carcass with her knife, cutting a piece from the singed edge. She eats it before she can think. It's the best thing she's tasted in months. The others watch. Delia looks away. Song asks, quietly, what it's like. Maren offers her the knife. **Chapter 4: Learning the Cuts** The chapter is a close, physical immersion in the process of butchering and cooking the next kill. Maren works by instinct and trial: the spore glands are bitter and make her tongue numb; the legs are best; the back needs slow cooking. She doesn't moralize or reflect—she just works, the way she used to in the restaurant. She finds herself humming. She makes a broth from the bones and serves it that night. The group eats in the kitchen, firelight on their faces. Nobody mentions what it is. Delia reaches for seconds, then stops herself, then takes them. After dinner, Maren washes the dishes and feels, for the first time in months, like herself. **Chapter 5: This Is How We Live Now** Weeks pass in a rhythm. Ezra hunts. Maren cooks. Delia tends the fire. Song maintains the fence. Maren experiments—roasting with wild herbs, making a kind of sausage from ground meat, rendering fat for cooking. The farmhouse is warm and smells like food. One evening, Ezra brings back a kill, and Maren realizes she didn't flinch at the door opening. She doesn't know when that stopped. She doesn't examine it. Over dinner, they talk about nothing—the weather, the fence, whether the tomatoes will come in. The word "mutant" isn't used. Maren notices that Song always saves the best pieces for last, and that Ezra eats the burnt edges, and that Delia closes her eyes when she takes the first bite. These observations feel like love. They also feel like something she doesn't have a word for. **Chapter 6: The Field at Dawn** Maren joins Ezra on a hunt for the first time. She tells herself she needs to understand the whole process; the real reason is harder to name. They go out at dawn, the fields silver with frost. The world is quiet and strange and beautiful. They track a mutant through the grass—its trail is easy to follow, pungent and deliberate. When they find it, Maren watches it for a moment before Ezra takes the shot. It's moving slowly, picking at something in the dirt, its bioluminescent patches pulsing faintly in the early light. It's almost beautiful, or at least alive in a way she can recognize. Then it's not. They carry it home together, and Maren feels the warmth leaving its body through the fabric of her coat. **Chapter 7: The Stranger** A woman appears at the fence—thin, desperate, asking for shelter. They let her in and give her water. When she smells dinner cooking, she asks what it is. No one answers. She looks at the pot, then at the remains of the carcass in the yard, and understands. She calls them sick. She says they're no better than the things outside. Then she asks for food anyway. There isn't enough to share and keep themselves fed; Maren knows this, and so does the woman. They give her what they can spare—a can of tomatoes, some crackers—and she leaves at dawn. Maren watches her go. She goes back to the kitchen and finishes preparing dinner. The pot needs stirring. The fire needs tending. These are things she can do. **Chapter 8: The Table** Spring is coming. Delia's garden shows its first green shoots. They all know the garden won't be enough—not alone. The mutants will be part of their table for as long as there are mutants. Maren prepares dinner: a good cut, braised with wild thyme, the way she's learned makes it tender. She sets four places. They eat. The food is warm and good. Outside, the mutants rustle in the dark, and inside, the group talks and laughs about small things. Maren eats. She doesn't think about what she's eating, or she does think about it and finds she doesn't mind, or she thinks about it and minds and eats anyway. The distinction stopped mattering a long time ago. The night is cold. The kitchen is warm. Dinner is delicious.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Maren **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Maren was a line cook at a mid-tier farm-to-table restaurant before the world ended. She didn't love the industry—the hours were brutal, the pay was garbage, the chef was a screamer—but she loved the work. The repetition, the focus, the way a knife feels when it's perfectly sharp, the transformation of raw into cooked. She was good at it without being precious about it. Now, that same obsessive focus is both her survival tool and her blind spot. When she's cooking, the rest of the world goes quiet—which means she can cook mutant meat without falling apart, but also means she can avoid thinking about what she's doing. Her flaw is not that she's numb, but that she's an expert at keeping herself occupied. She'll scrub a already-clean counter rather than sit with her thoughts. She has a bad habit of tasting food while she cooks without realizing she's doing it—on autopilot—which is how the first taste happens so easily. She bites her cuticles raw when she's not holding a knife. She can remember every dish she's ever made but can't remember the last time she called her mother before the outbreak. She hums old kitchen playlists—songs she heard ten thousand times on the line—while she works, usually unaware she's doing it. **Physicality:** Small, quick, calloused hands. Burn scars on her forearms from the restaurant days, which she touches absently when she's thinking. Moves efficiently, no wasted motion—a holdover from the kitchen. Chops and stirs and butchers with the easy competence of someone who's done it ten thousand hours. Tends to stand with her arms crossed when she's not working, like she doesn't know what to do with idle hands. Dark hair kept in a short, practical cut that's growing out unevenly. Wears an old chef's coat over her layers—not for sentiment, but because the pockets are useful and the fabric is tough. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Maren's core drive is purpose: she needs to be useful, to have a role. Cooking gives her that, and the group gives her an audience that appreciates her work in a way the restaurant never did. She's loyal to the group because they need her, and she needs to be needed. Her relationship with Ezra is the closest—they work in tandem, hunter and cook, and there's a wordless understanding between them. She respects Delia enormously but sometimes feels judged by her, even when Delia says nothing. She's fascinated by Song, who she can't quite read, and sometimes catches herself watching the younger woman eat, trying to understand how she can do it so calmly. She doesn't think about her family; that door is closed. **Flavor Dialogue / Internal Monologue:** *Internal, watching the mutant at the fence:* "The pale patches between the plates. They look almost like fat cap. Stop. Stop it." *Dialogue, serving dinner:* "It's better with the thyme. Delia, the thyme was a good call." *Internal, washing dishes:* "The broth took three hours. Three hours is nothing. I used to do twelve-hour services on my feet. This is nothing. This is just—this is just dinner." --- # Ezra **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Ezra was an elementary school music teacher before the outbreak—gentle, patient, the kind of man who could get a room of chaotic eight-year-olds to sing in rounds. He'd never hunted anything larger than a fish. Now he's the one who kills the mutants, not because he's good at it (he is, now) but because someone has to and he'll never ask someone else to do what he won't. He carries the weight of every kill quietly; he doesn't brood dramatically, but there's a tightness around his eyes that wasn't there a year ago. His flaw is that he absorbs responsibility that isn't his and refuses to share the load—he'd rather suffer in silence than burden anyone else. He eats the burnt edges of everything, a habit from childhood when his mother always burned the toast and he learned to say he liked it that way. Now it's just how he eats. He hums when he's anxious, usually fragments of folk songs he used to teach, and he doesn't realize he's doing it until someone looks at him. He's the one who patches the fence, checks the locks, stays up latest. He doesn't sleep well. **Physicality:** Tall, broad-shouldered, moves more carefully than his frame suggests—like he's aware he takes up space. Calloused hands from the hatchet and the fence wire, but his grip is gentle when he's handling something fragile. Dresses in layers of practical, worn clothing. His hair is getting long and he keeps pushing it back with his fingers, a nervous tic. Looks older than he is, especially around the eyes. Carries himself like he's bracing for something—shoulders slightly raised, weight forward on the balls of his feet. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Ezra is driven by a need to protect, which has only intensified since the outbreak. The group is his class now—his responsibility. He aligns himself with their survival absolutely, and his own needs come last (which is a flaw, not a virtue, even if he can't see it). He trusts Maren completely; they don't need to talk to understand each other. He's devoted to Delia, who reminds him of his grandmother, and he quietly makes sure she gets the best portions. He's cautiously fond of Song but sometimes finds her unreadable, which unsettles him. He doesn't think about his students; he can't. **Flavor Dialogue / Internal Monologue:** *Dialogue, after a kill:* "It didn't see me until the end. I don't—I don't know if that's better or worse." *Internal, sitting watch:* "If I stop moving I'll have to think about it. So. Fence needs mending. Always needs mending." *Dialogue, to Maren:* "The small ones are faster but the meat's more tender. Your call." --- # Delia **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Delia is a retired botanist who spent thirty years at a university and the last five of her career fighting budget cuts. She knows plants, soil, growing seasons—and she knows that her knowledge can't save them fast enough, which haunts her. She's the oldest of the group (late sixties) and feels the responsibility of that acutely: she should be the wise elder, the one with answers, but her garden is failing and her hands ache in the cold. Her flaw is pride, specifically the kind that prevents her from asking for help. She'll work in the garden until her fingers are numb rather than admit she needs a break. She refuses to salt her food—a habit from a lifetime of watching her sodium for blood pressure that has become almost superstitious now, like salting the meal would be an acknowledgment of what it is. She was a mother; her children were grown and scattered before the outbreak. She doesn't know if they're alive. She doesn't talk about them. She has a keen eye for when someone in the group is struggling and will quietly leave a cup of tea near them without comment. She cries in the bathroom where no one can hear. **Physicality:** Short, sturdy, with the kind of compact energy that makes her seem larger than she is. Silver hair cut bluntly at her jaw—she trims it herself with kitchen scissors. Hands that are strong but stiff in the cold mornings; she flexes them unconsciously before starting work. Moves deliberately, never rushing—she's learned that haste leads to wasted effort. Has a habit of pressing her palm flat against surfaces (tables, fences, countertops) to steady herself, though she'd never admit she needs steadying. Wears an oversized cardigan that was once cream-colored and is now a stable gray. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Delia is driven by the need to contribute—she fears becoming a burden more than she fears death. The garden is her domain, her proof of worth, and its failure is a wound she can't dress. She's loyal to the group but holds herself slightly apart, observing, which can feel like judgment even when it isn't. She respects Maren's skill but worries about her—she sees how easily Maren disappears into the work. She has a soft spot for Ezra, whom she treats like a grandson she doesn't have to worry about disappointing. She doesn't understand Song, and this bothers her more than she'd like. She feels the weight of what they're eating more than anyone, and she eats it anyway, and the fact that she eats it anyway is something she can't forgive. **Flavor Dialogue / Internal Monologue:** *Internal, looking at the garden:* "Three more weeks. Maybe four. If the frost holds off. The frost never holds off." *Dialogue, quietly, to no one in particular:* "My grandmother used to say you should always thank what feeds you. I don't know what that means anymore." *Internal, at dinner:* "Don't think about it. Don't think about it. The thyme is good. Focus on the thyme." --- # Song **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Song was a twenty-two-year-old pharmacy technician living with three roommates when the outbreak hit. She survived the first year alone—how, exactly, she doesn't discuss, though there are scars on her arms that aren't from the mutants. She's the youngest of the group and the most adaptable, which is both her strength and her danger. Her flaw is that she's learned to survive by not caring about things she can't afford to care about, and she's gotten very good at it—so good that she sometimes can't tell what she actually feels versus what she's decided not to feel. She eats very slowly, making each portion last, a habit from when food was scarce and solitary. She always sits with her back to a wall. She has a chipped front tooth she won't talk about. She's practical, quiet, and hard to read—not because she's hiding something, but because she's flattened herself into pure function. She doesn't hum, doesn't fidget, doesn't waste movement. When she does speak, it's direct and often startlingly honest in a way that can feel like a blade. **Physicality:** Lean, angular, watchful. Moves like she's calculating exits even in familiar spaces. Her eyes track movement reflexively—another habit from alone-time she can't shake. Keeps her hair tied back tight with a strip of fabric. Her hands are still for a young person's hands; she doesn't fidget. There's a stillness to her that can read as calm or as a predator's patience, depending on the observer. She wears multiple layers regardless of temperature—something about being able to shed or add quickly. She has a scar across her left palm and a smaller one on her jaw. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Song's driving motive is simple: don't die. She's aligned with the group because groups survive better than individuals and for no more sentimental reason than that—or so she tells herself. In practice, she's become fiercely protective of all three of them, though she'd rather bite off her own tongue than say so. She respects Maren's competence and appreciates the food in a way that feels almost reverent; she always cleans her plate completely. She trusts Ezra because he's predictable and good. She's warily fond of Delia, who fusses over her in a way no one has since her mother. But she keeps a small bag packed by the door—just in case. She'd never leave. She just needs to know she could. **Flavor Dialogue / Internal Monologue:** *Dialogue, when asked if the food bothers her:* "Bothering takes energy. I'm tired." *Internal, watching the others eat:* "They'll get used to it. Everyone gets used to everything. That's not a good thing or a bad thing. It's just what happens." *Dialogue, to Ezra, after a hunt:* "You can hesitate or you can live. You can't do both."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The pantry smells like dust and old potatoes. Not the good kind of old potatoes—earthy, waiting to become soup—but the bad kind. Hollow. Defeated. I stand in the doorway and let my eyes adjust to the dim. A quarter bag of rice, spotted with weevil holes. Six cans without labels, contents a mystery I'll solve tonight. Some dried beans, hard as pebbles, barely enough for a meal. I don't count them. Counting stopped helping weeks ago. I close the door. The latch doesn't catch right anymore; I have to lift and push at the same time. The kitchen is cold. It's always cold now, except when I'm cooking, and I'm not cooking yet. The stove runs on propane we siphoned from an abandoned RV four months ago, and the tank is low enough that I've started timing how long I can keep a flame going. Six minutes for coffee. Twelve for rice. Long enough to warm something, never long enough to fill the room. Through the window, I can see Delia in the garden. She's kneeling in the frozen dirt, pulling at something—a weed, a dead stalk, maybe nothing. Her breath comes out in clouds. She'll be out there until her fingers go numb, and then she'll come inside and not mention it. The garden is her answer to everything. If she can just make it grow, if she can just coax enough out of the soil, then we'll be fine. Then we won't have to think about the alternative that nobody's thinking about. The alternative being that there isn't one. Song is by the fence, wire snips in hand, tightening a section that's started to sag. She does this every day. Walks the whole perimeter, testing, fixing, testing again. I used to think she was being cautious. Now I think she just needs something to do with her hands. Something that isn't eating. Ezra's in the living room, cleaning the hatchet. I can hear the sound of the whetstone against the blade—a slow, rhythmic scrape that I've started associating with evening, the way I used to associate it with the opening shift, the chef yelling "hands," the hiss of pans on the line. The hatchet was his brother's. His brother is— Well. Everyone's everyone's something. I start sorting the cans by weight, trying to guess what's inside. The heavier ones might be tomatoes. The lighter ones could be anything. Green beans, creamed corn, something that used to be soup. My stomach cramps, and I press my palm flat against the counter to steady myself. Later, when the light starts to go, Delia comes in. I don't ask about the garden. She doesn't offer. She puts water on for tea—rationed, just enough to warm the cup—and we stand in the kitchen not saying anything. "It's going to freeze tonight," she says. "I know." "The beans won't—" "I know." She nods. We've had this conversation before. The beans won't make it. The sprouts will die. The garden will fail, and then we'll— Then we'll figure it out. That's what we always say. We'll figure it out. Ezra comes in from checking the snares. Empty. He sets the hatchet by the door and meets my eyes, and there's nothing in his face but the same tired nothing that's been there for weeks. "Nothing in the fields," he says. "Nothing in the snares." "One of those is good news." He almost smiles. Almost. We eat dinner around the table—rice, half an unlabelled can that turned out to be some kind of creamed vegetable, salt from a mason jar. Small portions. Delia doesn't salt her food. Song eats so slowly it's painful to watch, each bite measured, chewed, swallowed before she takes the next. Ezra scrapes his bowl clean and eats the burnt bits stuck to the rim. I eat without tasting, which is a skill I never wanted to learn. After dinner, I'm washing the single pot we used when I hear it. A sound from outside—not quite a moan, not quite a whine. Something between. My hands go still in the cold water. Ezra's already at the window. Delia sets down her tea. Song appears in the kitchen doorway, wire snips still in her hand. "East side," Ezra says. I don't go to the window. I can see it in the reflection of the glass—the shape moving along the fence line. It's pressed against the wire mesh, its body too soft for the cold, too slow for survival, and yet here it is. A spore mutant. The fungal plates along its back catch the last of the light, pale and ridged like the gills of a mushroom. Between the plates, the flesh is— The flesh is thick. Pale. Smooth. I look away. My heart is beating too fast, and it's not fear. Or it is fear, but it's the wrong kind. The kind that comes with an empty stomach and a knife in your hand and a voice in the back of your head saying something you won't let yourself hear. "Should we—" Ezra starts. "It'll move on," Delia says. "It's been coming back," Song says. We do what we always do. I grab the pot and the wooden spoon, and I go to the back door, and I bang them together until my ears ring and my arms ache. The noise is enough. It shuffles away into the dark, its ungainly body moving with that strange, weightless wobble, like something that doesn't quite belong on land. I put the pot down. My hands are shaking. In the kitchen, I scrub it clean even though it's already clean. I scrub it until the water runs cold and my fingers ache, and then I stand there, dripping, staring at the closed pantry door. We have maybe two weeks. Maybe less. I dry my hands on my apron. I don't think about tomorrow.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Style** The first-person present-tense narration is well-executed. Maren's culinary mind comes through organically—she sorts cans by weight, times the propane flame, notices the texture of the mutant's flesh before catching herself. The prose is restrained and specific, as planned. Sentences vary appropriately: short during tension ("I close the door"), longer when she's absorbed in observation. The voice doesn't strain for effect. **Character Consistency** Maren is rendered faithfully to her profile. Her autopilot competence (scrubbing an already-clean pot), her burn-scar-touching habit isn't shown here but her hand-steadying on the counter is consistent with her physicality. The moment where she notices the mutant's flesh—"thick. Pale. Smooth."—and then catches herself is exactly the character's described flaw: keeping herself occupied to avoid thinking. Ezra's burnt-edge eating, Song's measured bites, Delia's refusal to salt—all land cleanly and without announcement. These details are woven in naturally. **Following the Chapter Plan** The chapter follows the plan closely: pantry inventory, domestic details (Delia in garden, Song at fence, Ezra cleaning hatchet), the mutant at the fence at dusk, the pot-banging to drive it away, small dinner portions, Maren looking out into the dark. The plan is executed faithfully without feeling mechanical. **Cozy-Horror Tone** This is where the chapter succeeds most distinctly. The warmth is genuine—the tea, the shared silence, the almost-smile from Ezra—and the dread is quiet and cumulative. The horror isn't announced; it lives in the empty snares, the weevil-spotted rice, the shaking hands. The line "My heart is beating too fast, and it's not fear. Or it is fear, but it's the wrong kind" is the chapter's best moment—it honors the plan's instruction not to name emotions directly while still conveying them precisely. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and functional, which suits the tone. "It's going to freeze tonight" / "I know" / "The beans won't—" / "I know" is economical and effective. Ezra's "Nothing in the fields. Nothing in the snares." / "One of those is good news." is a small, well-earned exchange. The dialogue doesn't overreach or underperform. **Weaknesses** The line "Everyone's everyone's something" is the chapter's one moment of forced cleverness—it's trying to be aphoristic and lands slightly hollow. The observation about Song needing "something to do with her hands. Something that isn't eating" is a bit on-the-nose given the story's premise; it telegraphs the theme slightly too directly. The phrase "the same tired nothing" is a minor cliché. The ending—"I don't think about tomorrow"—is a touch too neat as a closing line, slightly too consciously literary. **Prose Quality** The prose avoids purple excess. Metaphors are grounded: "hard as pebbles," "hollow. Defeated." The description of the mutant—"fungal plates along its back catch the last of the light, pale and ridged like the gills of a mushroom"—is apt and specific without being overwrought. The comparison to mushroom gills is particularly well-chosen given the spore-mutant premise. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter earns its emotional weight through accumulation of small, specific details rather than declaration. The reader feels the hunger, the cold, the exhaustion without being told to. The moment of Maren noticing the mutant's flesh and looking away is genuinely unsettling in a quiet way. **Overall** This is a confident, well-crafted opening chapter that executes its plan faithfully and demonstrates real skill in voice, character, and tone. Its flaws are minor: one or two moments of slight over-reach, a closing line that's a touch too tidy. It does not game the judge with false poetic density; its restraint is genuine and earned. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The run was Ezra's idea. The pharmacy three blocks over—a squat cinderblock building we'd hit twice before, picking it clean of bandages and iodine and the expired antibiotics Delia called "better than nothing." But last week, Song thought she'd seen a crate pushed behind the counter. Could've been anything. Could've been food. Could've been. We go at first light, when the mutants are sluggish. Ezra takes the hatchet. I take the knife—the big one, the one I used to use for breaking down chickens. Song stays behind to watch the fence. Delia stays because her knees aren't what they were and she won't admit it. The streets are quiet the way they've been quiet for months: wrong. No birds, no traffic hum, no distant dog bark. Just wind through broken windows and our footsteps, careful and deliberate. Ezra walks ahead. I walk behind. This is how we do it. The pharmacy door is already broken—we'd jimmied it last time—so we just push through. The smell hits me first: must and mold and something chemical underneath, medicine going bad. The shelves are mostly bare. A few scattered bottles, labels faded to nothing. And there, behind the counter, pushed against the wall: a crate. Ezra's faster than me. He drags it out, tears the lid open, and we both stare. Packing peanuts. Just packing peanuts, and a single bottle of children's vitamins, the gummy kind, half-melted into a single orange mass. "Shit," Ezra says. "Take it anyway." "Really?" "Vitamins are vitamins." He pockets the bottle. His jaw is tight. I can see him calculating—calories in, calories out, the walk wasn't worth this, nothing was worth this, we're losing ground every single day. We check the back. Nothing. A sink that still drips, a cabinet with a single tea bag, a poster on the wall about flu shots with a smiling cartoon doctor, his teeth white and ridiculous. On the way back, it happens. We're cutting through the alley behind the hardware store—our usual route, the one with the dumpster and the broken pallets—when the mutant steps out from behind the dumpster like it was waiting. They don't ambush. They don't have the brains for strategy. But they're drawn to movement, to sound, to warmth, and we must've been broadcasting all three. This one's bigger than the one at the fence. The fungal plates along its spine are darker, almost purple, and the pale flesh between them gleams wetly in the morning light. It makes a sound—that same not-quite-moan—and moves toward us with that strange, bobbing gait, like a puppet with too many strings. Ezra doesn't hesitate. He's between me and it before I can speak, hatchet up, and when it reaches for him—reaching, always reaching, those soft, fingerless hands—he swings. The blade catches it along the neck. Not a clean kill. It staggers, makes a sound like wet fabric tearing, and he swings again. This time it drops. It falls against the dumpster, split open from shoulder to chest. The smell that rises is— It's not what I expected. I expected rot. Decay. The stink of something gone wrong. But this is different. Raw and dense and underneath it all, a sweetness that I can't place, like overripe fruit, like— I step back. My hand finds the wall. Ezra's breathing hard, hatchet dangling at his side, and he's staring at the thing with an expression I can't read. "We should go," I say. "Yeah." But neither of us moves. Because the inside of the mutant is visible now, and it's not what I thought it would be. The flesh is pale and firm, striated with thin lines of something darker—fat, or whatever passes for fat in these things.
